nodes.title: Linear Transformations
nodes.content: A linear transformation is a function T that maps vectors to vectors while preserving addition and scalar multiplication: T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v) and T(cv) = cT(v) for any scalar c. In finite dimensions, every linear transformation can be represented as a matrix A, so that T(v) = Av.

teaches.label: Linear transformation
teaches.description: A vector-to-vector function preserving addition and scalar multiplication

teaches.label: Matrix representation
teaches.description: Every finite-dimensional linear transformation can be written as Av

nodes.title: Geometric View of Transformations
nodes.content: Geometrically, a linear transformation can stretch, rotate, reflect, or project vectors. Consider a 2x2 matrix acting on vectors in the plane. Most vectors change both their direction and magnitude. But some special vectors only get scaled -- their direction stays the same (or reverses). These are the eigenvectors.

teaches.label: Eigenvector intuition
teaches.description: Special vectors whose direction is unchanged by a transformation

requires.label: Linear transformation
requires.description: A vector-to-vector function preserving addition and scalar multiplication

nodes.title: Formal Definition of Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues
nodes.content: Formally, a nonzero vector v is an eigenvector of A if Av = λv for some scalar λ. The scalar λ is called the eigenvalue associated with v. The equation says: applying A to v has the same effect as simply multiplying v by λ. If λ > 1, the vector gets stretched. If 0 < λ < 1, it gets compressed. If λ < 0, it gets reversed and scaled. If λ = 0, the vector is sent to the zero vector -- it lies in the null space of A.

teaches.label: Eigenvector definition
teaches.description: Nonzero v such that Av = λv

teaches.label: Eigenvalue definition
teaches.description: The scalar λ in Av = λv

teaches.label: Eigenvalue interpretation
teaches.description: λ > 1 stretches, 0 < λ < 1 compresses, λ < 0 reverses, λ = 0 nullifies

requires.label: Matrix representation
requires.description: Every finite-dimensional linear transformation can be written as Av

nodes.title: Finding Eigenvalues: The Characteristic Equation
nodes.content: To find eigenvalues, we rearrange Av = λv to (A - λI)v = 0. For this to have a nonzero solution v, the matrix (A - λI) must be singular, which means its determinant is zero. The equation det(A - λI) = 0 is called the characteristic equation. For an n×n matrix, this is a polynomial of degree n in λ, called the characteristic polynomial. Its roots are the eigenvalues.

teaches.label: Characteristic equation
teaches.description: det(A - λI) = 0, whose roots give eigenvalues

teaches.label: Characteristic polynomial
teaches.description: Degree-n polynomial in λ for an n×n matrix

requires.label: Eigenvector definition
requires.description: Nonzero v such that Av = λv

requires.label: Eigenvalue definition
requires.description: The scalar λ in Av = λv

nodes.title: Worked Example: 2×2 Matrix
nodes.content: For example, consider A = [[2, 1], [1, 2]]. The characteristic equation is det([[2-λ, 1], [1, 2-λ]]) = (2-λ)² - 1 = λ² - 4λ + 3 = (λ-1)(λ-3) = 0. So the eigenvalues are λ₁ = 1 and λ₂ = 3. To find the eigenvector for λ₁ = 1, we solve (A - I)v = 0, giving [[1, 1], [1, 1]]v = 0, so v₁ = [-1, 1] (or any scalar multiple). For λ₂ = 3, we get v₂ = [1, 1].

teaches.label: Eigenvalue computation
teaches.description: Concrete procedure for finding eigenvalues and eigenvectors

requires.label: Characteristic equation
requires.description: det(A - λI) = 0, whose roots give eigenvalues

nodes.title: Symmetric Matrices and the Spectral Theorem
nodes.content: The eigenvectors of a symmetric matrix are always orthogonal. This is not a coincidence -- it follows from the Spectral Theorem, which states that every real symmetric matrix can be diagonalized by an orthogonal matrix. In other words, there exists an orthogonal basis consisting entirely of eigenvectors. This is one of the most important results in linear algebra.

teaches.label: Spectral theorem
teaches.description: Real symmetric matrices have orthogonal eigenvector bases

teaches.label: Orthogonal eigenvectors
teaches.description: Symmetric matrices always have orthogonal eigenvectors

requires.label: Eigenvector definition
requires.description: Nonzero v such that Av = λv

requires.label: Eigenvalue definition
requires.description: The scalar λ in Av = λv

nodes.title: Diagonalization
nodes.content: Diagonalization simplifies computation enormously. If A = PDP⁻¹ where D is diagonal (containing the eigenvalues) and P's columns are the eigenvectors, then A^n = PD^nP⁻¹. Computing D^n is trivial -- just raise each diagonal entry to the nth power. This makes eigendecomposition essential for computing matrix powers, exponentials, and solving systems of differential equations.

teaches.label: Diagonalization
teaches.description: A = PDP⁻¹ decomposition using eigenvectors and eigenvalues

teaches.label: Matrix powers via diagonalization
teaches.description: A^n = PD^nP⁻¹ makes powers trivial to compute

requires.label: Eigenvector definition
requires.description: Nonzero v such that Av = λv

requires.label: Eigenvalue definition
requires.description: The scalar λ in Av = λv

nodes.title: Eigenvalues in Quantum Mechanics
nodes.content: In physics, eigenvalues appear everywhere. In quantum mechanics, observable quantities (energy, momentum, angular momentum) correspond to eigenvalues of Hermitian operators. The state of a system is a vector in Hilbert space, and measuring an observable collapses the state to an eigenvector of that operator, with the measurement yielding the corresponding eigenvalue. The discrete spectrum of eigenvalues explains why atomic energy levels are quantized.

teaches.label: Quantum mechanical eigenvalues
teaches.description: Observables are eigenvalues of Hermitian operators; measurement collapses to eigenstates

requires.label: Eigenvector definition
requires.description: Nonzero v such that Av = λv

requires.label: Eigenvalue definition
requires.description: The scalar λ in Av = λv

nodes.title: Principal Component Analysis
nodes.content: In data science, principal component analysis (PCA) finds the eigenvectors of a covariance matrix. The eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue points in the direction of greatest variance in the data. The second eigenvector (orthogonal to the first) points in the direction of second greatest variance, and so on. By projecting data onto the top k eigenvectors, PCA achieves dimensionality reduction while preserving the most important structure in the data.

teaches.label: Principal Component Analysis
teaches.description: Dimensionality reduction via eigenvectors of covariance matrix

requires.label: Eigenvalue definition
requires.description: The scalar λ in Av = λv

requires.label: Orthogonal eigenvectors
requires.description: Symmetric matrices always have orthogonal eigenvectors

nodes.title: Eigenvalues and Stability
nodes.content: The connection between eigenvalues and stability is fundamental. For a system of linear differential equations dx/dt = Ax, the eigenvalues of A determine the behavior of solutions. If all eigenvalues have negative real parts, the system is stable -- perturbations decay exponentially. If any eigenvalue has a positive real part, the system is unstable. This criterion extends to nonlinear systems near equilibrium points via linearization, making eigenvalue analysis the workhorse of stability theory.

teaches.label: Eigenvalue stability criterion
teaches.description: Sign of eigenvalue real parts determines system stability

requires.label: Eigenvalue definition
requires.description: The scalar λ in Av = λv

requires.label: Eigenvalue interpretation
requires.description: λ > 1 stretches, 0 < λ < 1 compresses, λ < 0 reverses, λ = 0 nullifies
